title: Developing International Applications
text: Developing International Applications Uniface supports Unicode and many other character sets, which makes it possible to develop international applications in different languages. It enables you to take locale into account when displaying, sorting, and manipulating data, and to adjust times to the client time zone. It also allows you to configure an application for right-to-left languages. Types of International Applications You can make the following kinds of international applications in Uniface. Multilingual applications—a multilingual Uniface application runs in a Unicode environment. The application can display many different languages in a single component, and accept inputs from different languages into one field. Specific language applications—a national language application can be built directly in the language’s platform. For example, you can build a Simplified Chinese application in a Simplified Chinese Microsoft Windows environment, or an Arabic application in an Arabic Windows environment. Generic language applications—an application can be deployed in multiple language environments. Using Uniface’s language variation technique (for details, see Libraries of Runtime Objects ), a language-independent Uniface application can be built. When you deploy it in a specific language environment, its user interface appears in that language. For example, in a Cyrillic Windows environment, the application appears in Russian. If you deploy the same application in a Japanese Windows, it appears in Japanese. Character Sets To support data entry and display in various languages, Uniface supports multiple character sets, which can be divided into two categories: Installable character sets—during Uniface installation, you can select a character set in the Character set selection screen. These are the installable character sets. Unicode—Uniface's internal character set. Language and Locale Different languages and regions use different conventions when sorting and displaying data. For example, English speaking countries use the period as a decimal separator and the comma as 1000 delimiter ( 123,456.89 ), whereas many European countries do the reverse ( 123.456,89 ). The term locale is commonly used to designate the combination of language and country or region, and the locale determines the default behavior for: Sorting data Formatting numbers Formatting date and time Formatting currency Switching between uppercase and lowercase Case-sensitive and case-insensitive pattern matching in strings Setting the time zone Uniface enables you to control and configure the locale and associated behavior using ProcScript functions, assignment settings, and display formats. Overview The documentation in this collection provides detailed information about Uniface-supported character sets and various issues related to developing international Uniface applications.
